findings.title: TaskRACI.userId has no FK relation — orphaned RACI rows, no cascade on user deletion
findings.code_evidence:
model TaskRACI {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId    String
  userId    String
  role      RACIRole
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())

  // Relations
  task Task @relation(fields: [taskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([taskId, userId, role])
  @@map("task_raci")
}
findings.description: TaskRACI stores a userId column and a composite unique constraint on (taskId, userId, role), but there is NO Prisma relation connecting userId to the User table. There is no `user User @relation(...)` in TaskRACI, and the User model (lines 56-97) has no back-relation to TaskRACI. This means: (1) PostgreSQL has no FK constraint on task_raci.user_id — the DB will happily accept a non-existent userId; (2) when a User is deleted, their RACI rows remain in the table as orphaned records with a dangling userId; (3) no onDelete behavior is enforced. Compare with TaskAssignee (lines 360-374) which correctly declares both `task Task @relation(...)` and `user User @relation(...)` with `onDelete: Cascade`.
findings.root_cause: The `user` relation side was omitted from TaskRACI when the model was created, leaving userId as a plain String with no FK backing.
findings.impact: After a User deletion (soft or hard), stale RACI rows survive referencing a non-existent userId. Queries joining task_raci on users will silently return no validator/responsible data. If userId is reused (UUID collision is astronomically unlikely but app logic may reuse logins), wrong user gets RACI assignment. No cascade means security-sensitive RACI data (RESPONSIBLE, ACCOUNTABLE) is never cleaned up.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add the FK relation on TaskRACI and the back-relation on User:

// In model TaskRACI, add:
  user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

// In model User, add to the relations block:
  taskRaci TaskRACI[]

Then generate and apply a migration:
  pnpm run db:migrate

The migration will add a FK constraint `REFERENCES users(id)` on task_raci.user_id and a cascade delete trigger consistent with the TaskAssignee pattern.
acceptance_criteria: Prisma schema validates without error after adding the relation
psql: `\d task_raci` shows a FK constraint on user_id REFERENCES users(id)
Deleting a user whose userId appears in task_raci removes those rows (or the FK constraint fires the intended behavior)
User.taskRaci back-relation is accessible from Prisma client queries
findings.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM information_schema.referential_constraints WHERE constraint_name LIKE '%task_raci%user%';"
findings.notes: No DAT-xxx annotation exists for this gap. TaskAssignee at lines 360-374 is the direct comparator — identical join-table structure but correctly wired.

findings.title: TaskDependency.dependsOnTaskId has no index — cascade delete on Task triggers seq-scan
findings.code_evidence:
model TaskDependency {
  id              String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId          String
  dependsOnTaskId String
  createdAt       DateTime @default(now())

  // Relations
  task          Task @relation("TaskDependencies", fields: [taskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  dependsOnTask Task @relation("DependentTasks", fields: [dependsOnTaskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([taskId, dependsOnTaskId])
  @@map("task_dependencies")
}
findings.description: The composite `@@unique([taskId, dependsOnTaskId])` creates an index with `taskId` as the leading column. Queries and FK checks driven from the `dependsOnTaskId` side (e.g., 'which tasks depend on task X?', or the PostgreSQL FK cascade when deleting a Task referenced as dependsOnTask) cannot use this index and will perform a full seq-scan on task_dependencies. As tasks accumulate, this becomes a lock/latency hotspot on every Task deletion or dependency lookup from the 'dependency target' side.
findings.root_cause: Only the forward-direction unique index was declared; no reverse index was added for the `dependsOnTaskId` FK column.
findings.impact: Every `DELETE FROM tasks WHERE id = $1` that has rows in task_dependencies (as either taskId or dependsOnTaskId) will seq-scan the table on the dependsOnTaskId side. At scale (thousands of tasks with dependency chains), this produces full-table locks during cascade deletes, potentially blocking the entire task mutation path.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a reverse index:

  @@index([dependsOnTaskId]) // reverse-lookup: tasks that depend on a given task

Migration: pnpm run db:migrate
acceptance_criteria: psql: `\d task_dependencies` shows an index on dependsOnTaskId
EXPLAIN on `SELECT * FROM task_dependencies WHERE "dependsOnTaskId" = $1` uses an index scan
findings.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'task_dependencies';"
findings.notes: Pattern is consistent with DAT-029 which explicitly added @@index([serviceId]) to UserService, @@index([userId]) to ProjectMember, and @@index([userId]) to EventParticipant for the same reason. TaskDependency was missed.

findings.title: UserSkill.skillId has no standalone index — cascade delete on Skill triggers seq-scan
findings.code_evidence:
model UserSkill {
  userId      String
  skillId     String
  level       SkillLevel
  validatedBy String?
  createdAt   DateTime   @default(now())
  updatedAt   DateTime   @updatedAt

  // Relations
  user  User  @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  skill Skill @relation(fields: [skillId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@id([userId, skillId])
  @@map("user_skills")
}
findings.description: The composite PK `@@id([userId, skillId])` creates a B-tree index with `userId` as the leading column. Looking up 'all users with skill X' (the reverse direction) or firing the `onDelete: Cascade` FK enforcement when deleting a Skill row requires scanning the entire `user_skills` table on the skillId column. The DAT-029 annotation explicitly added @@index([serviceId]) to UserService for the same structural reason; UserSkill was not covered.
findings.root_cause: The composite PK only covers the userId-leading direction; no standalone index was declared for skillId.
findings.impact: Deleting a Skill (e.g., when decommissioning an obsolete skill) causes PostgreSQL to seq-scan user_skills to cascade-delete dependent UserSkill rows. For organizations with many users and skills, this can cause table-level lock escalation. Querying 'which users have skill X' (a workload-planning query) also seq-scans.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a reverse index:

  @@index([skillId]) // DAT-029 pattern: reverse-lookup 'all users with skill X'

Migration: pnpm run db:migrate
acceptance_criteria: psql: `\d user_skills` shows an index on skillId
EXPLAIN on `SELECT * FROM user_skills WHERE "skillId" = $1` uses an index scan
findings.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'user_skills';"
findings.notes: UserSkill.validatedBy (line 767) is also a plain String? with no FK relation — it stores a user ID without referential integrity. This is a secondary concern at lower severity given there's no back-relation or cascade needed, but it means validator identity can silently go stale.

findings.title: ProjectMember.role is a free String while DAT-012 promoted all other closed-set strings to enums
findings.code_evidence: role       String // Chef de projet, Membre, Observateur...
findings.description: The comment enumerates a closed set of values ('Chef de projet, Membre, Observateur') yet the column stays as String. DAT-012 explicitly promoted other closed-string fields (PredefinedTaskDuration, DayPeriod, AssignmentCompletionStatus, RecurrenceType, AppSettingsCategory) to enums. The '...' suffix in the comment may signal intentional extensibility, but the known values form a finite, semantically distinct set. Without an enum, the application layer is the sole guard against invalid role strings, and the DB can store arbitrary values.
findings.root_cause: ProjectMember was not reviewed during the DAT-012 enum-promotion pass, or the '...' was read as 'this is open-ended'.
findings.impact: Invalid/misspelled role strings (e.g., 'Chef de project', ' Membre') can be persisted; filtering by role is case/value-sensitive with no compile-time guard. Cross-system consistency (e.g., a second service querying project_members) cannot rely on a bounded set.
findings.suggested_fix:
Define an enum and migrate the column:

enum ProjectMemberRole {
  CHEF_DE_PROJET
  MEMBRE
  OBSERVATEUR
}

Then in ProjectMember: `role ProjectMemberRole`. Add a data migration to normalise existing values before applying the constraint.
acceptance_criteria: project_members.role column is a PostgreSQL enum type
Existing rows contain only normalized enum values
findings.notes: Confidence medium because the '...' comment may indicate deliberate extensibility. If the set is intentionally open (e.g., admin-configurable), this finding should be dropped.

findings.title: TimeEntry lacks a CHECK ensuring exactly one of (userId, thirdPartyId) is non-NULL
findings.code_evidence:
model TimeEntry {
  id           String       @id @default(uuid())
  userId       String?
  thirdPartyId String?
  declaredById String?
  projectId    String?
  taskId       String?
  date         DateTime     @db.Date
  hours        Decimal      @db.Decimal(5, 2) // hours, max 999.99 ; DAT-005
findings.description: TimeEntry is polymorphic: a row belongs to either an internal User (userId) or an external ThirdParty (thirdPartyId). Both are nullable, so the DB can store rows where both are NULL (orphaned, no owner) or both are non-NULL (double-attributed). The application layer likely enforces 'exactly one', but the schema does not express this invariant. Prisma cannot express CHECK constraints in PSL, but they can be added via a raw SQL migration (the project already uses this pattern, e.g., the partial unique index for LeaveBalance at line 696-698).
findings.root_cause: Prisma PSL has no CHECK constraint syntax; the invariant was left to the application layer without a raw-SQL migration guard.
findings.impact: A bug or direct DB write can create time entries with no owner (invisible to all user/third-party views) or double-owned entries (counted twice in aggregations). Both corrupt time-tracking reports and project budget calculations.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a raw SQL migration:

ALTER TABLE time_entries ADD CONSTRAINT time_entries_owner_xor
  CHECK (
    (user_id IS NOT NULL AND third_party_id IS NULL) OR
    (user_id IS NULL AND third_party_id IS NOT NULL)
  );

Document the constraint in schema.prisma with a comment (same pattern as the LeaveBalance partial-unique comment at line 696-698).
acceptance_criteria: psql: `\d time_entries` shows the CHECK constraint
INSERT with both userId and thirdPartyId non-NULL raises a constraint violation
INSERT with both NULL raises a constraint violation
findings.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid = 'time_entries'::regclass AND contype = 'c';"
findings.notes: This is the single representative CHECK-invariant finding. Other range invariants (progress 0-100, allocation 0-100, dayOfWeek 0-6) exist but are lower risk; this one can silently corrupt financial/time aggregations.

verified_clean: Float/Decimal: zero Float fields exist — every money/time/hours/balance field uses Decimal with @db.Decimal precision (DAT-005 annotations at lines 216, 323, 454, 619, 704). No IEEE-754 drift risk.

Business-key @@unique coverage: Service has @@unique([departmentId, name]); UserService has @@unique([userId, serviceId]); ProjectMember has @@unique([projectId, userId]); TaskAssignee has @@unique([taskId, userId]); PredefinedTaskAssignment has @@unique([predefinedTaskId, userId, date, period]); TeleworkSchedule has @@unique([userId, date]); ProjectSnapshot has @@unique([projectId, date]); LeaveBalance has @@unique([userId, leaveTypeId, year]) + partial-unique index for NULL userId case documented at lines 696-698.

onDelete behavior: Project→Task is Restrict (prevents orphaned tasks on hard-delete, DAT-007); ProjectSnapshot is Restrict; TimeEntry→Project is Restrict; Document→Project is Restrict. User→Department is Restrict; manager FKs use SetNull throughout. Leave→User is Cascade; LeaveBalance→User is Cascade. AuditLog→User is NoAction (intentional: immutability trigger blocks SetNull, documented at lines 1223-1226). All reviewed and appropriate.

Enums cover all known closed value sets: TaskStatus, Priority, ProjectStatus, MilestoneStatus, LeaveStatus, HalfDay, LeaveType, ActivityType, ThirdPartyType, HolidayType, SchoolVacationZone, SchoolVacationSource, RACIRole, SkillCategory, SkillLevel, PredefinedTaskDuration, DayPeriod, AssignmentCompletionStatus, RecurrenceType, AppSettingsCategory — all present.

FK indexes: all FK columns that are not leading columns of a composite index have explicit @@index declarations (DAT-011 pattern applied consistently across Department, Service, Project, Epic, Milestone, Task, LeaveBalance, Leave, TeleworkRecurringRule, PredefinedTask, PredefinedTaskRecurringRule, Holiday, SchoolVacation, Event, Document, Comment, PasswordResetToken, Role).

Nullable vs NOT NULL: roleId on User is intentionally nullable during RBAC V4 backfill transition (documented at lines 35-37); deletedAt nullable is intentional soft-delete marker (DAT-026); all other required business fields are NOT NULL.
LeaveTypeConfig: missing onDelete on Leave→LeaveTypeConfig (line 635) — leave type cannot be cascade-deleted while leave rows reference it; Prisma defaults to Restrict which is appropriate here.
ProjectMember.allocation Int? (0-100 range): absence of CHECK is consistent with other range fields; low risk as it's a reporting hint not a financial calculation.
AuditLog immutability: DB-level trigger enforced (documented lines 1219-1226), append-only enforced at privilege layer (TOOL-DEPLOY-001 two-role split lines 10-18).
RBAC V4 Role model: code is @unique, templateKey is NOT NULL (documented lines 1196-1198), @@index([templateKey]) and @@index([isDefault]) present.
RefreshToken: tokenHash is @unique, expiresAt and userId are indexed — security-sensitive fields properly constrained.
PasswordResetToken: token is @unique, expiresAt and userId,usedAt indexed (DAT-028).
Document soft-delete: deletedAt indexed (DAT-025), uploadedBy FK with SetNull on User deletion.
User email: @db.VarChar(254) per RFC 5321, @unique for Prisma compatibility, LOWER() functional unique index documented as added via raw SQL (DAT-015 comment lines 27-29).
User.login: @unique present, unbounded String acceptable (login is not email-length-constrained).
Holiday.date: @@unique([date]) prevents duplicate public holidays on the same date.
SchoolVacation: @@unique([name, zone, year]) prevents duplicate vacation periods.

TaskDependency self-reference (taskId == dependsOnTaskId): not protected by CHECK in DB; application layer guards this. Risk is low (circular dependency detection is the real concern, not self-reference).
